questions.question: What does CMSIS stand for?
questions.explanation: CMSIS (Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard) is an ARM standard providing a hardware abstraction layer for Cortex-M processors. It standardizes access to core peripherals (NVIC, SysTick, MPU) across vendors.

questions.question: CMSIS-Core provides direct register access to vendor-specific peripherals (UART, SPI, ADC).
questions.explanation: CMSIS-Core only covers ARM-designed core peripherals: NVIC (interrupts), SysTick (timer), SCB (system control), MPU (memory protection). Vendor peripherals require vendor-specific HAL/drivers or CMSIS-Driver standard.

questions.question: In CMSIS, the function to set an interrupt priority is NVIC_Set___().
questions.explanation: NVIC_SetPriority(IRQn, priority) sets the priority of an interrupt. Lower numbers = higher priority. NVIC_EnableIRQ(), NVIC_DisableIRQ(), and NVIC_SetPriority() are the three most-used CMSIS NVIC functions.

questions.question: What does this CMSIS code do?
questions.explanation: Writing to AIRCR requires the key 0x5FA in VECTKEY. PRIGROUP=5 means: of the 8 priority bits, 2 are preemption priority (4 levels) and 6 are sub-priority. This controls nested interrupt behavior.

questions.question: Match each CMSIS component to its purpose:

right: Standardized access to Cortex-M core registers and NVIC
Optimized signal processing library (FFT, filters, matrix ops)
Generic RTOS API (portable across FreeRTOS, RTX, Zephyr)

questions.explanation: CMSIS is a family of standards: Core (hardware abstraction), DSP (math library), RTOS2 (OS abstraction), NN (ML inference), Driver (peripheral API), Pack (software distribution).

questions.question: Interrupt-driven UART receive stops working after priority configuration change. Find the bug:
options: PRIORITYGROUP_0 means ALL 8 bits are sub-priority, 0 bits preemption — UART can't preempt anything
SysTick at priority 4 is now higher than UART at 5, blocking UART
HAL function mixed with CMSIS functions causes conflict
questions.explanation: PRIORITYGROUP_0 gives 0 preemption bits and all sub-priority bits. With no preemption levels, NO interrupt can preempt another — all have effective preemption priority 0. If any higher-priority ISR is running, UART ISR waits indefinitely.
